title: Concentration of solutions — how much is dissolved?
examTip: Always convert cm³ to dm³ (divide by 1000) before using c = n/V. A 250 cm³ solution is 0.25 dm³. Getting this wrong is the most common way to drop marks on concentration questions.
introTips: Remember: 1 dm³ = 1 litre = 1000 cm³. Practise the conversion until it's automatic.
In titrations, always write out the mole ratio from the balanced equation before doing the calculation.
You need to be able to convert between mol/dm³ and g/dm³ by multiplying or dividing by Mr.
intro: Why does a teaspoon of salt barely flavour a bowl of soup, but a teaspoon in a glass of water makes it undrinkable? The answer is concentration — how much solute is crammed into a given volume. Understanding concentration isn't just useful in chemistry; it underpins medicine (drug dosages), environmental science (pollutant levels), and food technology. In the lab, being precise about concentration is the difference between a reaction working and getting a dangerously wrong result.

keyPoints.heading: What concentration means — and its units
keyPoints.content:
Concentration is the amount of solute dissolved in a given volume of solution. A more concentrated solution has more solute particles packed into the same volume. There are two units you need at GCSE:

mol/dm³ (molar concentration) — moles of solute per dm³ of solution. A 1 mol/dm³ solution contains 1 mole of solute per litre.

g/dm³ (mass concentration) — grams of solute per dm³ of solution.

Key volume conversions: 1 dm³ = 1 litre = 1000 cm³. A 500 cm³ solution is 0.5 dm³. A 250 cm³ solution is 0.25 dm³. Always convert cm³ to dm³ before using the concentration formula.
writeBullets: Concentration — amount of solute dissolved per unit volume of solution
mol/dm³ — moles of solute per dm³ of solution (molar concentration)
g/dm³ — grams of solute per dm³ of solution (mass concentration)
1 dm³ = 1 litre = 1000 cm³ — always convert cm³ to dm³ before calculating
Higher concentration = more solute particles in same volume = stronger solution

keyTerms.def: The amount of solute dissolved per unit volume of solution; measured in mol/dm³ or g/dm³
The substance that dissolves in a solvent to make a solution
Cubic decimetre — equal to 1 litre or 1000 cm³; the standard volume unit for concentration calculations

keyPoints.memTip: 1 dm³ = 1 litre = 1000 cm³. If you see cm³, always divide by 1000 before you start calculating.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The cm³ to dm³ conversion is the single most common source of errors. Drill this conversion until it's a reflex.

keyPoints.heading: The concentration equation: c = n ÷ V
keyPoints.content:
The equation linking concentration, moles, and volume is: c = n ÷ V where c = concentration (mol/dm³), n = moles (mol), V = volume (dm³). Rearrangements: n = c × V, V = n ÷ c.

Worked example 1: 0.5 mol NaOH dissolved in 250 cm³ of solution. What is the concentration?
Convert: 250 cm³ ÷ 1000 = 0.25 dm³. c = 0.5 ÷ 0.25 = 2 mol/dm³.

Worked example 2: How many moles are in 100 cm³ of 0.1 mol/dm³ HCl?
Convert: 100 cm³ ÷ 1000 = 0.1 dm³. n = 0.1 × 0.1 = 0.01 mol.

Worked example 3: What volume of 2 mol/dm³ NaOH contains 0.04 mol?
V = 0.04 ÷ 2 = 0.02 dm³ = 20 cm³.
writeBullets: c = n ÷ V — concentration equals moles divided by volume in dm³
n = c × V — moles equals concentration multiplied by volume in dm³
V = n ÷ c — volume equals moles divided by concentration
Always convert cm³ to dm³ (÷ 1000) before substituting into the formula
Units: c in mol/dm³, n in mol, V in dm³ — be consistent throughout

keyTerms.def: Symbol for volume in dm³ (always convert cm³ to dm³ first)

keyPoints.memTip: Concentration triangle: put c at top, n and V at the bottom. Cover what you want to find.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Some students forget to do the cm³ → dm³ conversion when the volume is given in cm³ in the question. Make it habit to write the conversion as an explicit step.

keyPoints.heading: Concentration in g/dm³ — and converting between units
keyPoints.content:
Mass concentration (g/dm³) = mass of solute in grams ÷ volume of solution in dm³.

To convert between mol/dm³ and g/dm³, use the molar mass (Mr):
• g/dm³ = mol/dm³ × Mr
• mol/dm³ = g/dm³ ÷ Mr

Worked example 1: A solution of NaOH (Mr = 40) has a concentration of 40 g/dm³. What is this in mol/dm³?
c = 40 ÷ 40 = 1 mol/dm³.

Worked example 2: What is the mass concentration (g/dm³) of a 0.5 mol/dm³ solution of glucose (Mr = 180)?
c(g/dm³) = 0.5 × 180 = 90 g/dm³.

Worked example 3: 5.85 g of NaCl (Mr = 58.5) is dissolved in 500 cm³. What is the concentration in mol/dm³?
n = 5.85 ÷ 58.5 = 0.1 mol. V = 500 ÷ 1000 = 0.5 dm³. c = 0.1 ÷ 0.5 = 0.2 mol/dm³.
writeBullets: g/dm³ = mass concentration — grams of solute per dm³ of solution
To convert mol/dm³ to g/dm³: multiply by Mr
To convert g/dm³ to mol/dm³: divide by Mr
40 g/dm³ NaOH (Mr = 40) = 40 ÷ 40 = 1 mol/dm³
0.5 mol/dm³ glucose (Mr = 180) = 0.5 × 180 = 90 g/dm³

keyTerms.def: Concentration expressed in g/dm³ — grams of solute per dm³ of solution
Concentration expressed in mol/dm³ — moles of solute per dm³ of solution

keyPoints.memTip: Multiply by Mr to go from mol/dm³ to g/dm³. Divide by Mr to go from g/dm³ to mol/dm³.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA may give concentration in g/dm³ and ask you to work in mol/dm³ — remind students to convert first.

keyPoints.heading: Titrations — using concentration to find an unknown
keyPoints.content:
A titration is an experiment where you find the exact volume of one solution needed to neutralise another. From this you can calculate an unknown concentration. Method for a titration calculation:
1. Write the balanced equation and identify the mole ratio.
2. Calculate moles of the known solution (n = c × V in dm³).
3. Use the mole ratio to find moles of the unknown solution.
4. Calculate concentration of unknown (c = n ÷ V).

Worked example: 25.0 cm³ of NaOH solution is neutralised by 20.0 cm³ of 0.1 mol/dm³ HCl.
Equation: NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O (ratio 1:1).
n(HCl) = 0.1 × (20.0 ÷ 1000) = 0.1 × 0.02 = 0.002 mol.
Ratio 1:1 → n(NaOH) = 0.002 mol.
c(NaOH) = 0.002 ÷ (25.0 ÷ 1000) = 0.002 ÷ 0.025 = 0.08 mol/dm³.
writeBullets: Titration — measures exact volume of one solution needed to neutralise another
Step 1: write balanced equation and identify mole ratio
Step 2: find moles of known solution using n = c × V (V in dm³)
Step 3: use mole ratio to find moles of unknown
Step 4: find concentration of unknown using c = n ÷ V (V in dm³)
NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O: always a 1:1 mole ratio

keyTerms.def: A technique for finding the exact volume of one solution required to react completely with another
The reaction of an acid and a base to form a salt and water
The ratio of moles of substances given by coefficients in the balanced equation

keyPoints.memTip: Titration calculation: known solution → moles → ratio → moles → concentration of unknown. Four steps, always the same order.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Titration calculations frequently appear on higher-tier papers. Make sure students always convert cm³ to dm³ for BOTH volumes.

0.question: 0.2 mol of NaCl is dissolved in 400 cm³ of water. What is the concentration in mol/dm³?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Convert: 400 cm³ ÷ 1000 = 0.4 dm³. c = 0.2 ÷ 0.4 = 0.5 mol/dm³.
0.wrongFeedback: Remember to convert cm³ to dm³ first: 400 ÷ 1000 = 0.4 dm³. Then c = n ÷ V = 0.2 ÷ 0.4 = 0.5 mol/dm³.

2.question: A 2 mol/dm³ solution of H₂SO₄ (Mr = 98) — what is this concentration in g/dm³?
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right! To convert mol/dm³ to g/dm³: multiply by Mr. 2 × 98 = 196 g/dm³.
2.wrongFeedback: To convert mol/dm³ to g/dm³, multiply by Mr: 2 × 98 = 196 g/dm³.

3.question: In a titration, 25.0 cm³ NaOH is neutralised by 10.0 cm³ of 0.5 mol/dm³ HCl (1:1 ratio). What is the concentration of NaOH?
3.correctFeedback: Well done! n(HCl) = 0.5 × 0.01 = 0.005 mol. Ratio 1:1 → n(NaOH) = 0.005 mol. c = 0.005 ÷ 0.025 = 0.2 mol/dm³.
3.wrongFeedback: Work through the steps: n(HCl) = 0.5 × (10/1000) = 0.005 mol. Ratio 1:1 → 0.005 mol NaOH. c = 0.005 ÷ (25/1000) = 0.2 mol/dm³.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to convert cm³ to dm³ before using c = n/V — divide cm³ by 1000 every single time before substituting.
Using volume in cm³ for one substance and dm³ for another in the same calculation — be consistent throughout.
In titrations, forgetting to write the balanced equation and check the mole ratio — not all acid-base reactions are 1:1.
Confusing solute and solvent — concentration refers to the amount of solute (the thing dissolved), not the solvent (the liquid doing the dissolving).
Not converting g/dm³ to mol/dm³ when the question needs moles — always check which unit the question is asking for.
examTips: In any concentration question, write the conversion step explicitly: e.g., '250 cm³ ÷ 1000 = 0.25 dm³'. This earns a mark and prevents errors.
For titration calculations, always state the balanced equation and the mole ratio — examiners specifically look for this.
If asked to convert between mol/dm³ and g/dm³, multiply by Mr (to get g/dm³) or divide by Mr (to get mol/dm³).
Double-check your answer is a sensible concentration — typical lab concentrations are between 0.01 and 5 mol/dm³.
revisionCardBullets: Concentration = amount of solute per unit volume of solution
c = n ÷ V (V must be in dm³)
n = c × V; V = n ÷ c
Always convert cm³ to dm³ (÷ 1000) before calculating
Titration: find moles of known → use ratio → find moles of unknown → find concentration
NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O: 1:1 mole ratio
Titration steps: balance → n(known) → ratio → n(unknown) → c(unknown)
Check: is the answer a sensible concentration? (0.01–5 mol/dm³ is typical)
